coredata.dc:title: N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots promote charge separation of Bi4O5BrxI2−x solid solution and enhance visible light photocatalytic performance
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Xiaoming
Li, QianGen
Zhu, Wei
Li, Xibao
Guo, Yanhong
coredata.dc:description: Design and synthesis of photocatalysts with high charge separation and transfer efficiency are of considerable significance in environmental remediation. Herein, N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) decorated Bi4O5BrxI2−x solid solution was synthesized by temperature controlled in-situ coprecipitation method. The light absorption and absorption edge gap were improved significantly because of the formation of solid solution Bi4O5BrxI2−x, as well as N, S-GQDs was uniformly dispersed in the Bi4O5BrxI2−x nanosheets with diameter of about 10 nm, which enriched surface structure of Bi4O5BrxI2−x. Due to the enrichment electron effect of N,S-GQDs, the electron transferred from N,S-GQDs to Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 and enriched on the N,S-GQDs surface, resulting in efficient interface charge transfer and separation and the formation of superoxide radicals. These prominent advantages bring improved photocatalytic activity of the N, S-GQDs/ Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 for efficient removal of phenol. It displayed the optimal photocatalytic phenol degradation rate of 97.04% under visible light irradiation, which was 3.81 times higher than that of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 solid solution. This work could provide new insight into enhancing the photocatalytic performance of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 solid solution by graphene quantum dots modification and provide a strategy for photocatalytic phenol degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi4O5BrxI2-x solid solution
Charge carriers transfer
N, S-GQDs
Photocatalysis

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The preparation of Bi4O5BrxI2−x solid solution
Capture of active species and quantitative determination of free radical ·O2 -
Surface chemical composition and functional groups
Optical response performance and N2 adsorption desorption analysis

This work has received the finical support from, the Project of Department of Science and Technology of Shaanxi Province ( 2021GY-124 , 2022QFY07-03 ) and Postgraduate Education Innovation Project of Yan’an University ( YCX2021106 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patters of Bi4O5BrXI2−X，(b) XRD patters of Bi4O5BrXI2−X at 2θ= 20°–40° (c) XRD patters of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2,NSBBI-5,NSBBI-10,NSBBI-15 and NSBBI-20.
(a, d) TEM of NSBBI-15, (b) HR-TEM of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2, (c) HR-TEM of NSBBI-15, (e-l) EDS of of NSBBI-15.
Calculated band structures of Bi4O5I2(a), Bi4O5Br2(b), Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2(c), the total and partial density of state of Bi4O5I2(d), Bi4O5Br2(e), Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2(f).

(a) The photocatalytic degradation rate of phenol of the as-prepared samples, (b) Kinetic fitting curve of photocatalytic degradation of phenol of the as-prepared samples, (c) UV-Vis absorption spectra of photocatalytic degradation of phenol of NSBBI-15, (d) Apparent rate constants of photocatalytic degradation of phenol of as-prepared samples, (e) The degradation rate of phenol over NSBBI-15 in cycle experiment, (f) XRD patters of NSBBI-15 before and after four cycles.

(a) Transient photocurrent response spectrum of as-prepared samples, (b) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of as-prepared samples, (c) Photoluminescence spectra of NSBBI-15 and Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2, (d) Mott-Schottky curve of as-prepared samples.

(a)Photocatalytic degradation of phenol over NSBBI-15 with the addition of difference scavenger, (b) Absorption spectrum of NBT consumed by NSBBI-15 under difference time, (c) Variation of NBT concentration and ·O2− concentration.

Work function of (a) Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2, (b) N, S-GQD/Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2, 3D charge density differences along a-axis view(c) and b-axis (d) of N-CQD/BiOI0.25Br0.75, in which yellow and blue assurances represents charge accumulation and depletion, respectively.

head.ce:title: N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots promote charge separation of Bi4O5BrxI2−x solid solution and enhance visible light photocatalytic performance

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang,330063, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Design and synthesis of photocatalysts with high charge separation and transfer efficiency are of considerable significance in environmental remediation. Herein, N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) decorated Bi4O5BrxI2−x solid solution was synthesized by temperature controlled in-situ coprecipitation method. The light absorption and absorption edge gap were improved significantly because of the formation of solid solution Bi4O5BrxI2−x, as well as N, S-GQDs was uniformly dispersed in the Bi4O5BrxI2−x nanosheets with diameter of about 10 nm, which enriched surface structure of Bi4O5BrxI2−x. Due to the enrichment electron effect of N,S-GQDs, the electron transferred from N,S-GQDs to Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 and enriched on the N,S-GQDs surface, resulting in efficient interface charge transfer and separation and the formation of superoxide radicals. These prominent advantages bring improved photocatalytic activity of the N, S-GQDs/ Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 for efficient removal of phenol. It displayed the optimal photocatalytic phenol degradation rate of 97.04% under visible light irradiation, which was 3.81 times higher than that of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 solid solution. This work could provide new insight into enhancing the photocatalytic performance of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 solid solution by graphene quantum dots modification and provide a strategy for photocatalytic phenol degradation.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent, organic toxic pollutants were discharged by chemical industry and pharmaceutical factories, which seriously endangered the survival and health of human beings [1–3]. Due to some drawbacks of the traditional treatment methods, there was an urgent need to develop new treatment technologies. As an advanced oxidation technology, semiconductor photocatalysis had high efficiency, low cost and no secondary pollution, which attracted extensive attention [4–6]. However, the construction of efficient photocatalysts is a key in this field. Some semiconductor photocatalysts, such as oxygen based semiconductors (TiO2, CeO2, ZnO, et al.) hardly absorbed visible light and had low light absorption quantum efficiency[7–9]; Sulfur based semiconductor (CdS, ZnxCd1−xS, Bi2S3, et al.) and silver based semiconductors (Ag3PO4, AgX (X = Cl, Br, I), Ag2O et al.) had high recombination efficiency of photogenerated carrier due to their narrow band gap, and was prone to photo corrosion [10,11].

Bismuth based photocatalysts have attracted more and more attention because of their visible light response, excellent photocatalytic performance and non-toxic properties. Recently, bismuth based semiconductor photocatalysts, such as Bi2MoO6, Bi2WO6, Bi2S3, BiOIO3, BiFeO3, (BiO)2CO3 [12,13] have been reported for application to photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants [14], water decomposition [15], CO2 reduction and organic synthesis [16]. Among these materials, BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) has been widely studied. BiOX had a unique layered structure composed of [Bi2O2]2+ layer and double halogen atomic layer. The unique layered structure could form a built-in electric field, which effectively promote the separation of charge carriers and transfer from the bulk phase. In addition, the VB of BiOX was composed of O2p and Xnp (n = 3,4,5) orbitals, and CB was mainly composed of Bi6p orbitals [17]. Therefore, to regulate the energy band structure of BiOX by changing the stoichiometric ratio of bismuth to halogen atoms, the types of halogen atoms and solid solution strategy, which could form a uniform energy level, change the internal electrostatic field and promote the separation of photogenerated carriers [18,19]. Many bismuth halide oxide catalysts were synthesized by the strategy of bismuth-rich and solid solution, such as Bi4O5X2, Bi3O4X, Bi5O7X, Bi24O31X10, BiOClxI1−x, BiOBrxI1−x, BiOClxBr1−x, Bi4O5BrxI2−x, the photocatalytic activity has been significantly improved [20–22].

Although the strategy of bismuth-rich and solid solution can enhance light absorption and regulate the absorption edge gap. However, the enhanced performance was limited. In order to further improve the performance of BiOX, morphology control [23,24], doping [25], active site construction and heterojunction construction were adopted [26,27]. Generally, photogenerated electron hole pairs were formed in almost a few femtoseconds, while the migration of photogenerated electron hole pairs need hundreds of picoseconds. Therefore, the rapid recombination of photogenerated electron holes was prone to occur, which greatly limited the photocatalytic performance [28]. Therefore, it was necessary to explore an efficient strategy to improve the carrier separation efficiency and photocatalytic performance.

Recently, Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted great attention because of good solubility, photoelectric properties, chemical stability, low toxicity and simple synthesis route [29]. GQDs was a novel carbon material composed of graphene nanosheets with a size of less than 10 nm [30]. It had excellent property of electron storage and transfer properties and unique up-conversion photoluminescence. GQDs have been widely used in photovoltaics, bioimaging, and sensors. Many GQDs based photocatalysts have been prepared, for instance, GQDs/g-C3N4 [31], GQDs/Bi2S3 [32], GQDs/Bi2MoO6 [33]and so on. Especially, compared with GQDs, N, S-doped graphene quantum dots had lower work function and better optical properties, and could be excited by long wavelength light and converted into ultraviolet or shorter wavelength light [34,35]. Thus, introducing N,S-doped graphene quantum dots into the field of photocatalysis is a good choice. Due to the characteristics of electron storage and electron transfer, N,S-GQD could be used as electron separation centers on the catalyst surface. The separation of photogenerated carriers was promoted through electron tunneling. The unique up-conversion photoluminescence performance could broaden the light absorption range of the catalyst.

Therefore, it was of great significance to modify bismuth-rich solid solution BiOX with N, S-GQDs promoted the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers and enhanced the photocatalytic degradation. Up to date, the research on N, S-GQDs decorated Bi4O5BrxI1−x solid solution has not been reported. In paper, a novel N, S-GQDs/Bi4O5BrxI1−x heterojunction photocatalyst was constructed by hydrothermal-low temperature assisted synthesis way. The structural composition, optical and optical properties were studied detailedly. The photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared samples was evaluated by the visible light degradation phenol. Based on UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, active substance capture experiment and electron spin resonance analysis, the possible mechanism of enhancement photocatalysis was proposed.

N, S-GQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method. 0.23 g citric acid and 0.21 g thiourea were added to 4 mL N, N-dimethylformamide and ultrasonic for 30 min, so the citric acid and thiourea were completely dissolved to form a uniform and transparent solution. Then, the solution was transferred to a 25 mL high-pressure reactor and maintained at 180 ℃ for 8 h. After cooling the room temperature, ethanol was added to the solution and maintained at 10,000r / min for 20 min to extract the final product. The supernatant was then collected and dialyzed in deionized water (MWCO 2000) for 24 h to remove a small amount of impurities. Finally, the supernatant was dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h to obtain N, S-GQDs powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of Bi4O5BrxI2−x solid solution
ce:section.ce:para: Bi4O5BrxI2−x solid solution was simply prepared by one-step oil bath method. 2 mmol of Bi(NO3)3.5 H2O was added into 80 mL 0.1 M mannitol solution and stirred for 30 min to form A solution. At the same time, 2 mmol of KX (x = Br, I; where Br: I = 2:0, 1.6:0.4, 1.2:0.8, 0.8:1.6, 0.4:1.6, 0:2) was added to 20 mL 0.1 M mannitol to form B solution. Then, B solution was added dropwise into A solution and continue magnetic stirring for 30 min. Subsequently, the mixed solution was continually stirred in 90 ℃ oil bath for 6 h. After cooling the room temperature, the precipitation was collected, and washed with deionized water and absolute ethanol, dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h.

2 mmol of Bi(NO3)3.5 H2O was added to 80 mL 0.1 M mannitol solution and stirred magnetically for 30 min to form A solution. Meanwhile, 2 mmol of KX (X = Br, I; where Br:I = 0.8:1.2) was added to 20 mL 0.1 M mannitol, and then 0.4 mg/mL of N,S-GQDs solution (5 mL, 10 mL, 15 mL, 20 mL) was added for ultrasound to form B solution. Then, B solution was added dropwise into A solution and continue magnetic stirring for 30 min. Afterwards, the mixed solution was continually stirred in 90 ℃ oil bath for 6 h. After cooling the room temperature, the precipitation was collected, and washed with deionized water and absolute ethanol, dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h. The N, S-GQDs modified Bi4O5BrxI2−x solid solution was labeled NSBBI-5, NSBBI-10, NSBBI-15 and NSBBI-20. The preparation process of samples as shown in Scheme 1 (S. M.).

The phase and structure of the as-prepared samples was determined by X-ray powder diffraction instrument (XRD-7000, Shimadzu, Japan), Cu-Kα Ray, Ni filter, 40 kV voltage and 30 mA current, λ = 0.15418 nm, 2° min−1 scanning speed. The morphology of the as-prepared samples was measured by scanning electron microscope (JSM-6700, JEOL, Japan). The high resolution transmission electron microscope of the as-prepared samples was measured by transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). The elemental composition and chemical state of the samples were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (PHI-5400, PE, USA). The UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectrum (UV-Vis DRS) of the as-prepared samples were determined by UV-Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu, Japan), BaSO4 as the reference standard, 200–800 nm of scanning range. The specific surface area and pore size distribution of the as-prepared samples were characterized by automatic gas adsorption instrument (ASAP 2020, micromeritics, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured with fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-2700, Hitachi, Japan). The photoelectrochemical properties of the as-prepared samples were tested by electrochemical workstation (CH1660D, Chenhua, China) with three electrode battery, ITO glass was used as working electrode, Pt electrode and Ag/AgCl electrode were used as counter electrode and reference electrode, 0.5 mol/L Na2SO4 solution was used as electrolyte, and the light source was 300 W xenon lamp (λ ≥ 420 nm).

The photocatalytic degradation experiment was carried out in a 500 mL quartz reactor. The 300 W xenon lamp (PLE-SXE300/300UV, Beijing) with cut-off wavelength of 400 nm was used as the visible light source. 0.2 g catalyst was dissolved in 200 mL phenol solution with a concentration of 10 mg/L, and the solution was stirred for 60 min in the dark to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Then, turn on the xenon lamp, take samples at regular intervals, and determined the residual phenol concentration of by 4-amino antipyrine method. The detailed procedure of 4-amino antipyrine method was shown in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capture of active species and quantitative determination of free radical ·O2 -
ce:section.ce:para: P-benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA) and EDTA-2Na were used to capture ·O2 -, ·OH- and h+ free radicals, respectively. Using visible light as light source, electron spin resonance (ESR) was.

measured with DMPO (5,5′-dimethyl-1-pyrrolidine-n-oxide). The formation of ·O2 - was quantitatively determined by the conversion of 100 mL 2.5 × 10−5 mol/L NBT solution (0.1 g catalyst). The residual concentration of NBT was detected at 259 nm [36,37].

All the calculations were performed with the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) based on the density-functional theory (DFT). The side view of geometric structure of samples are shown in Fig. S1. The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with PBE functional was used for the work function, and a plane-wave expansion for the basis set with a cutoff energy of 450 eV was employed. Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2(001) were used to compute the work function. Geometry optimizations, density of state, band structures, differential charge density and work function were performed with a convergence threshold of 10−5 eV in energy and 0.025 eV Å−1 for the force. A vacuum of 15 Å along the Z-direction was used, which was large enough to minimize the interactions between periodic images.

The crystal structure of the prepared samples was analyzed by XRD. As shown in Fig. 1a, the prepared BiOIxBr2−x (x = 2, 1.6, 1.2, 0.8, 0.4, 0) showed similar monoclinic phase structure. When the molar ratio of KBr to KI was 2:0, the characteristic diffraction peak of the sample was corresponded to Bi4O5Br2 (PDF No 00–037–069)[38]. Meanwhile, no other diffraction peaks were observed, indicating that the purity of the prepared Bi4O5Br2 was very high. As for Bi4O5BrxI2−x, the content of I- increased with the decrease of x value, the diffraction peak of 2θ = 32.18° shifted to the direction of low angle, which could be obviously observed in the enlarged view of the XRD in the range of 20–40° (Fig. 1b). From Bragg equation 2dsinθ=nλ, when the doping of larger radius of heteroatom would lead to larger cell unit and lattice spacing, the diffraction peak of XRD would shift the direction of lower angle. Because the radius of I- (0.216 nm) was greater than that of Br- (0.195 nm), therefore, the XRD diffraction peak would shift to the direction of low angle when the position of Br- was replaced or occupied by I-. It indicated that Bi4O5BrxI2−x solid solution was formed by Bi4O5Br2 and Bi4O5I2, rather than mechanical mixing of Bi4O5Br2 and Bi4O5I2. When the molar ratio of KBr to KI was 0:2, the diffraction peaks at 28.8°, 31.5°, 45.2°, 49.4° and 54.4° corresponded to the (41−1), (020), (422), (006) and (811) crystal planes of Bi4O5Br2. The lattice parameters of Bi4O5BrxI2−x solid solution were shown in Tab. S1. It can be seen from Tab. S1 that the lattice parameters (a, b, c) of Bi4O5BrxI2−x showed an increasing trend with the increase of content I atom. The increasing trend of lattice parameters further proved that I atom was successfully introduced into the lattice of Bi4O5Br2. Generally, the infinite mutual solubility in binary metal solid solutions could be formed when the radius difference of metal ions was less than 15%. As for Bi4O5BrxI2−x, the difference between Br ion radius (0.195 nm) and I ion radius (0.216 nm) was less than 15% [39]. Therefore, a stable solid solution could be formed. As shown in XRD patters of N, S-GQDs/Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 (Fig. 1c) ed by N, S-GQDs. It illustrated that N, S-GQDs did not change the crystal structure of Bi4O5BrxI2−x solid solution.

The SEM of Bi4O5Br2, Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 and NSBBI-15 were shown in Fig. S2. As shown in Fig. S2a, b, Bi4O5Br2 and Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 was an irregular block structure formed by sheet stacking. From Figs. S1c and 2a, with the introduction of N, S-GQDs, the morphology of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 did not change. NSBBI-15 was composed of nanosheets, and N, S-GQDs dispersed in the nanosheets with diameter of about 10 nm. According to Fig. 2b, the lattice spacing of 0.309 nm and 0.261 nm was corresponded to (41−1) and (220) crystal planes of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 solid solution, respectively [40,41]. According to Fig. 2c, the lattice spacing of 0.309 nm corresponded to (41−1) crystal plane of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 solid solution, while lattice spacing of 0.280 nm corresponded to the (1120) crystal plane of graphite carbon, indicating that N, S-GQDs were successfully introduced into the surface of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 solid solution. In order to further confirm the composition of NSBBI-15, the EDS was carried out. As shown in Fig. 2(e-j), Bi, Br, I, O, C, N and S dispersed uniformly in the surface of NSBBI-15, which further conformed that N, S-GQDs were successfully introduced into Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface chemical composition and functional groups
ce:section.ce:para: The element composition and chemical state of prepared sample was analyzed by XPS. According to the XPS full spectrum (Fig. S3), the elements of Bi, Br, I, O, C, N and S were detected, which was agreed to the composition of NSBBI-15. In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Bi 4 f ( Fig. 3a), the binding energies at 159.50 eV and 164.81 eV were ascribed to Bi 4 f7/2 and Bi 4 f5/2 respectively, indicating that Bi existed in Bi4O5BrxI2−x as Bi3+ [42]. In the high-resolution XPS spectra of Br3d (Fig. 3b) and I3d (Fig. 3c), the binding energies at 69.52 eV and 68.48 eV were agreed to Br3d3/2 and Br3d5/2, and the binding energies at 630.30 eV and 618.84 eV were attributed to I3d3/2 and I3d5/2 [43,44]. The binding energies of 284.80 eV, 286.29 eV and 288.21 eV were corresponded to sp2 C in C-C, sp3 C in C-N and C = O (Fig. 3d) [45]. In the XPS high-resolution spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. 3e), the peaks located at 399.71 eV and 401.71 eV were belonged to pyrrole N(C-N-C) and graphite N or N-H bond [46,47]. In addition, the peaks at 159.13 eV and 164.40 eV were corresponded to S2p3/2 and SO in high-resolution XPS spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 3f) [33]. It was worth noting that the change of binding energy revealed the change of surface electron density. As for Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2, the binding energies of Br 3d, I 3d and Bi 4 f in NSBBI-15 increased significantly, while the binding energies of S 2p and N 1 s decreased with N, S-GQDs, indicating that there was a strong interaction between Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 and N, S-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical response performance and N2 adsorption desorption analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The light absorption capacity of the as-prepared sample was studied by UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-Vis DRS). From Fig. S4a, the absorption band of Bi4O5Br2 (Bi4O5BrxI2−x, x = 2) was 452 nm. With the decrease of x value, the absorption band redshifted, and the absorption of Bi4O5I2 redshifted to 550 nm. As shown in Fig. S4b, The Eg of Bi4O5I2, Bi4O5Br0.4I1.6, Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2, Bi4O5Br1.2I0.8, Bi4O5Br1.6I1.4, Bi4O5Br2 were 2.12 eV, 2.19 eV, 2.35 eV, 2.45 eV, 2.50 eV and 2.67 eV, respectively. Obviously, Bi4O5BrxI2−x solid solution decreased with the increase of I- content. From Fig. S4c, compared to Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2, the absorption intensity and absorption tail attenuation of NSBBI-15 in the full spectrum region were significantly enhanced. Thus, NSBBI-15 has stronger light absorption capacity. In order to understand the specific surface area and pore size distribution, the low-temperature nitrogen adsorption of NSBBI-15 was studied. According to Fig. S4d, the nitrogen adsorption desorption isotherm of NSBBI-15 was type IV, and the pore diameter was mainly distributed in 75 nm, indicating that there were both mesoporous in NSBBI-15. The BET specific surface area was 70.96 m2/g, which provided rich activation sites for the adsorption of organic pollutant.

The calculated band structures of Bi4O5I2, Bi4O5Br2 and Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 were shown in Fig. 4(a-c), the calculation methods and models were shown in Supporting Material. The calculated band gap of Bi4O5I2, Bi4O5Br2 and Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 were 2.36 eV, 2.73 eV and 2.49 eV, respectively, which were in accord with the variation trend of experimental values (~2.12 eV, ~2.67 eV, ~2.35 eV, respectively). The calculated total and partial density of state of Bi4O5I2, Bi4O5Br2 and Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 were shown in Fig. 4(e-f). The CB of Bi4O5I2, Bi4O5Br2 and Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 was composed of Bi 6p orbitals, the VB of Bi4O5I2 was composed of O 2p orbitals and I 5p orbitals, the VB of Bi4O5Br2 was dominated by O 2p orbitals and Br 4p orbitals, and the VB of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 was consisted of the O 2p orbitals, Br 4p orbitals and I 5p orbitals.

The photocatalytic performance of Bi4O5BrxI2−x and NSBBI-15 was evaluated by degrading phenol. The results are shown in Fig. 5. According to Fig. 5(a, b), the degradation rates of phenol over Bi4O5Br2, Bi4O5I2 were only 7.5% and 57.4% after 90 min of visible light irradiation. The solid solution was formed between Bi4O5Br2 and Bi4O5I2, the photodegradation efficiency of Bi4O5BrxI2−x was improved. The degradation rate of phenol over Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 was 73.0% after 90 min of visible light irradiation. While modified by N, S-GQDs, the photodegradation efficiency of phenol was greatly improved, and NSBBI-15 showed the best photocatalytic activity, the degradation rate of phenol reached 97.04% after 90 min of visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 5(c, d), the apparent rate constant (Kapp) of NSBBI-15 was 0.0404 min−1, which was 33.38 times that of Bi4O5Br2, 3.96 times that of Bi4O5I2 and 3.81 times that of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5e, the degradation rate of NSBBI-15 decreased from 97.04% to 92.1% after four cycles, which would be caused by incomplete recovery of the catalyst. The repeated experiment for three times for all the catalysts, and the result was shown in Fig. S5. After used three times, the photocatalytic efficiency of all prepared catalysts basically unchanged. Moreover, the degradation rate of phenol over NSBBI-15 for three times was 96.4%, 96.2% and 96.9% respectively. The XRD pattern of NSBBI-15 before and after at four cycles use was shown in Fig. 5f. The crystal structure of four cycles had not changed basically. Therefore, NSBBI-15 had excellent photocatalytic stability. The performance of N, S-GQDs modified Bi4O5BrxI2−X solid solution with previous studies in degrading pollutants was compared, and the result was shown in Tab. S2. It indicated that N, S-GQDs modified Bi4O5BrxI2−X solid solution had relatively good photocatalytic performance comparable to modification by rare earth metals and noble metals.

The transient photocurrent response spectrum of the as-prepared samples was shown in Fig. 6a. From Fig. 6a, the photocurrent density intensity of all of as-prepared samples had almost no change in on/off cycles of visible light, indicating that all of as-prepared samples had high light response stability. The intensity of photocurrent density of Bi4O5Br2, Bi4O5I2, Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 were 0.25 μA/cm2, 0.20 μA/cm2, 0.37 μA/cm2, respectively. the intensity of photocurrent density of NSBBI-15 was 0.95 μA/cm2, which was 3.8 times than that of Bi4O5Br2, 4.75 times t times than that of Bi4O5I2, and 2.56 times than that of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2. The higher intensity of photocurrent response indicated that more photogenerated carriers could be produced, which was benefit to improve the photocatalytic performance. the electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) of the as-prepared samples was shown in Fig. 6b. In general, the smaller the radius of the EIS, the smaller the migration resistance of photogenerated carriers. From Fig. 6b, the radius of the EIS of NSBBI-15 was the smallest. Consequently, the migration resistance of photogenerated carriers of NSBBI-15 was the smallest, and the interfacial charge transfer was the fastest. The photoluminescence spectra of NSBBI-15 and Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 was shown in Fig. 6c, According to Fig. 6c, the emission peak intensity of NSBBI-15 at 310 nm was lower than that of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2. It showed that NSBBI-15 had low recombination of photogenerated carrier in the process of photocatalysis. The Mott-Schottky (M-S) curve of Bi4O5Br2, Bi4O5I2, Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 was shown in Fig. 6d. From Fig. 6d, the estimated value of flat band potential (Efb) was − 0.76 eV, - 0.30 eV, - 0.50 eV vs Ag/AgCl. Due to the slope of the M-S curve was positive, Bi4O5Br2, Bi4O5I2, Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 were n-type semiconductor. As for n-type semiconductor, Efb was equal to the Fermi level (Ef), and the conduction band potential (ECB) was more negative 0.1–0.3 eV than Ef. Therefore, ECB(vs NEH) of Bi4O5Br2, Bi4O5I2, Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 could be determined by
E
CB
NEH , pH = 6.8
=
E
fb
Ag , AgCl
+ 0.199 − 0.2
[48], ECB(vs NEH) of Bi4O5Br2, Bi4O5I2, Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 was − 0.76 eV、− 0.30 eV、− 0.50 eV [49]. According to UV-Vis DRS, The Eg of Bi4O5Br2, Bi4O5I2, Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 was 2.67 eV, 2.12 eV and 2.35 eV. Therefore, the EVB of Bi4O5Br2, Bi4O5I2, Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 was + 1.91 eV, + 1.85 eV and + 1.82 eV, respectively. As a result, the energy level structure was shown in Fig. S6.

To understand the mechanism of photocatalytic degradation, isopropanol (IPA), p-benzoquinone (BQ) and disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetate (EDTA-2Na) were used to capture the free radicals ·OH, ·O2 - and h +, and the results were shown in Fig. 7a. As no addition of capture agent, the photocatalytic degradation rate of phenol was 97.04%. After the addition of IPA, the photocatalytic degradation rate of phenol decreased slightly, was 93.04%. With the addition of BQ and EDTA-2Na, the photocatalytic degradation rate of phenol decreased from 97.04% to 9.27% and 21.87%, respectively. The degradation effect was significantly inhibited, indicating that ·O2 - and h + played a major role in the degradation phenol. In addition, NBT could be used as a probe to detect the generation of ·O2 -, and the generation of ·O2 - was more than four times of the consumption of NBT. With the extension of irradiation time, the absorbance of NBT solution at 259 nm almost showed a linear downward trend (Fig. 7b, c). After 120 min of light irradiation, the generated ·O2 - reached 1.1564 × 10−5 mol/L, which indicated that a large amount of ·O2 - was produced in the process of photocatalysis.

The ESR spectrum of NSBBI-15 was shown in Fig. S7. According to Fig. S7, NSBBI-15 was irradiated with visible light, the signals of ·O2 - and ·OH- appeared in the ESR spectrum, and the signal intensities of ·O2 - and ·OH increased significantly with the extension of illumination time, which proved that ·O2 - and ·OH were produced in the process of photocatalysis. It was consistent with the active species capture experiment. The degradation pathway of phenol by NSBBI-15 was discussed by the High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC). Two intermediates of p-benzoquinones and carboxylic acids were produced in the process of photocatalytic degradation of phenol, which were basically consistent with our previous reports [17]. As shown in Fig. S8a, p-benzoquinone was generated after irradiation for 20 min, indicating that phenol was transformed into p-benzoquinone under the action of ·O2 -, and then oxidized into carboxylic acids. The concentration of p-benzoquinone remained almost unchanged within 40 min. Therefore, two possible degradation pathways for photocatalytic degradation of phenol was proposed (Fig. S8b): (1) In the presence of ·O2 -, phenol was directly oxidized into carboxylic acid, which were mineralized into CO2 and H2O [50]. (2) phenol was transformed into P-benzoquinone by ·O2 -, then P-benzoquinone were transformed into carboxylic acids, and finally into CO2 and H2O under the combined action of ·O2 -, ·OH and h + [51].

To furtherly understand the interface charge transfer between N, S-GQDs and Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2, the work function was estimated to obtain the electrostatic potential of Fermi levels. As shown in Fig. 8(a-b), the work function of (001) surface of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 and N, S-GQDs/Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 were 5.60 eV and 4.06 eV, respectively. Evidently, after the introduction of N, S-GQDs to BiOI0.25Br0.75, the work functions decreased. Therefore, the electrons were apt to escape from the catalyst surface and accelerated the transfer and separate of the photogenerated electrons. As a consequence, more ·O2 - and ·OH radicals would be produced and the photocatalytic activity was significantly improved. The 3D charge density differences of N, S-GQDs/Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 was shown in Fig. 8(c-d). The charge density differences mainly occurred at interface of N-GQDs/BiOI0.25Br0.75 after an equilibrium was achieved. The interface charge densities were redistributed with electron accumulation region (yellow surfaces) and electron depletion region (blue surfaces), respectively. Accordingly, the electrons could effectively transfer at the interface of N, S-GQDs and the (001) surface of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2.

Therefore, the possible mechanism of photocatalytic degradation over NSBBI-15 was shown in Fig. 9. The band gap of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 solid solution was 2.35 eV, under the irradiation of visible light, the electrons were excited and transferred from VB to CB. Simultaneously, an equal number of holes were generated in the valence band. Due to an internal electric field from N, S-GQDs to Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2, the were effectively separated and transferred. The ECB (−0.50 eV) of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 was more negative than Eθ(O2/·O2 -) (−0.33 eV) [52], which indicated that photogenerated electrons could react with O2 to produce ·O2 -. In addition, the EVB (+1.85 eV) of Bi4O5Br0.8I1.2 was more positive than Eθ(·OH/OH-) (+1.99ev) [53], which showed that ·OH was not oxidized by h +, but by electron multistep induced reduction of O2. The process accelerated the consumption of electrons and prefer to the separation of photogenerated carriers. Therefore, phenol molecules would be attacked by ·O2 - and ·OH and oxidized to CO2, H2O and other intermediates.
(1)
NSBBI − 15 + hv →
e
−
+
h
+
(2)

−
+
O
2
→ ·

(3)

+ 2
H

→ · OH +
OH
−
(5)
phenol +
· O
2
−
/ · OH /
h
+
→
CO
2
+
H
2
O + Intemediates

In this work, N, S-GQDs modified Bi4O5BrxI2−X solid solution photocatalyst was synthesized by a simple one-step temperature controlled in-situ coprecipitation method. The specific surface area, absorption intensity, photogenerated carrier separation efficiency of Bi4O5BrxI2−x was significantly improved due to the uniform dispersion of N, S-GQDs in the surface of Bi4O5BrxI2−x. Under visible light irradiation, the photocatalytic activity of N, S-GQDs/Bi4O5BrxI2−X was evaluated. NSBBI-15 had the best effect on photocatalytic degradation of phenol, and the removal effect of phenol was 97.04% within 90 min. The enhanced photocatalytic performance was attributed is due to the synergistic effect of the novel structure, which provided rich adsorption sites and appropriate energy band, as well as the separation and transfer of photogenerated charge hole pairs accelerated due to the electron enrichment effect of N, S-GQDs. This study could provide theoretical support for the enhancement of photocatalytic activity by the synergistic effect of solid solution and quantum dots, and provide new insights for the modification of Bi4O5BrxI2−x.

Xiaoming Gao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing. QianGen Li: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Wei Zhu: Software. Xibao Li: Investigation. Yanhong Guo: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has received the finical support from, the Project of Department of Science and Technology of Shaanxi Province (2021GY-124, 2022QFY07-03) and Postgraduate Education Innovation Project of Yan’an University (YCX2021106).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid synthesis of BiOBrxI1−x photocatalysts: insights to the visible-light photocatalytic activity and strong deviation from Vegard’s law
